        ```python
        from maspy import *

class DeliveryWorld(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.package_locations = {
            "depot": ["package1", "package2"],
            "location1": [],
            "location2": []
        }
        self.delivery_destinations = {
            "package1": "location1",
            "package2": "location2"
        }

    def pick_up_package(self, package_id):
        for location, packages in self.package_locations.items():
            if package_id in packages:
                self.package_locations[location].remove(package_id)
                return True
        return False

    def deliver_package(self, package_id, destination):
        self.package_locations[destination].append(package_id)

class Courier(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None, env=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.env = env
        self.add(Belief("delivered_count", (0,)))
        self.add(Belief("at_location", ("depot",)))
        self.add(Goal("deliver_packages", (2,)))

    @pl(gain, Goal("deliver_packages"), Belief("delivered_count", (Any,)))
    def start_delivery(self, src, delivered_count):
        if int(delivered_count[0]) == 0:
            self.add(Goal("pick_up_package"))

    @pl(gain, Goal("pick_up_package"), Belief("at_location", ("depot",)))
    def pick_up_package(self, src, at_location):
        package_id = self.env.package_locations["depot"][0]
        self.env.pick_up_package(package_id)
        self.add(Belief("current_package", (package_id,)))
        destination = self.env.delivery_destinations[package_id]
        self.add(Goal("travel_to", (destination,)))

    @pl(gain, Goal("travel_to"), Belief("at_location", (Any,)))
    def travel_to(self, src, goal_location, at_location):
        if goal_location != at_location:
            self.rm(Belief("at_location", (at_location,)))
            self.add(Belief("at_location", (goal_location,)))
        self.add(Goal("deliver_package"))

    @pl(gain, Goal("deliver_package"), Belief("current_package", (Any,)), Belief("at_location", (Any,)))
    def deliver_package(self, src, current_package, at_location):
        self.env.deliver_package(current_package[0], at_location)
        self.add(Belief("delivered_package", (current_package[0],)))
        self.rm(Belief("delivered_count", (Any,)))
        delivered_count = int(self.get(Belief("delivered_count"))[0]) + 1
        self.add(Belief("delivered_count", (str(delivered_count),)))
        if delivered_count < 2:
            self.add(Goal("pick_up_package"))
        else:
            self.stop_cycle()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    env = DeliveryWorld()
    courier = Courier(env=env)
    Admin().connect_to([courier], env)
    Admin().start_system()
        ```

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

TITLE: Defining Plans

To define plans it is also really simple, it only needs the @pl decoration. This decoration must contain the plan change {gain, lose or test}, the data that changed {Belief(s) or Goal(s)} and optionally a context needed to be true to execute the plan {Belief(s) or Goal(s)}.

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')
    changed_data: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal
    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)
    def foo(self,src, *changed_data.values, *context.values):
```

Resuming the the driver example, you can implement a plan the following way:

```py
from maspy import *

class Driver(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("park"))

    # This plan will be executed whenever the agent gains the goal "checkPrice"
    # Every plan needs at least self and src, plus the arguments from the trigger and context
    # for this plan, the context is the belief of a budget with wanted and max prices
    @pl(gain,Goal("checkPrice", Any),Belief("budget",(Any, Any)))
    def check_price(self, src, given_price, budget):
        ...

driver = Driver("Drv")
```

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

from maspy import *
import random as rnd
from time import sleep

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

class Manager(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agt_name=None):
        super().__init__(agt_name,read_all_mail=True)
        self.add(Belief("spotPrice",15,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Belief("minPrice",10,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("broadcast_price"))
        self.print("Manager initialized")
        self.end_counter = 0
    
    @pl(gain,Goal("broadcast_price"), Belief("spotPrice",Any))

        ```python
        from maspy import *

class MarketEnv(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("item", ("apple", 8, 1)))

    def reduce_stock(self, item, quantity):
        item_percept = self.get(Percept("item", (item, Any, Any)))
        if item_percept:
            current_price, current_stock = item_percept.values[1:]
            new_stock = max(0, current_stock - quantity)
            self.change(item_percept, (item, current_price, new_stock))

class Buyer(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget", (100, adds_event=False)))
        self.add(Goal("buy", ("apple",)))

    @pl(gain, Goal("buy", Any), Belief("budget", (Any,)))
    def request_item(self, src, item, budget):
        item_percept = self.env.get(Percept("item", (item, Any, Any)))
        if item_percept:
            price = item_percept.values[1]
            if budget >= price:
                self.send(src, achieve, Goal("process_purchase", (item,)))
            else:
                self.print(f"Not enough budget to buy {item}")

    @pl(gain, Goal("process_purchase", Any))
    def process_purchase(self, src, item):
        item_percept = self.env.get(Percept("item", (item, Any, Any)))
        if item_percept and item_percept.values[2] > 0:
            self.env.reduce_stock(item, 1)
            self.add(Belief("owns", (item,)))
            self.print(f"Bought {item}")
        else:
            self.print(f"Item {item} out of stock")

class Seller(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Goal("sell"))

    @pl(gain, Goal("sell"))
    def sell_item(self, src):
        item_percept = self.env.get(Percept("item", ("apple", Any, Any)))
        if item_percept:
            price = item_percept.values[1]
            self.send(src, tell, Belief("price", (price,)))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    env = MarketEnv()
    buyer = Buyer(agent_name="Buyer")
    seller = Seller(agent_name="Seller")
    Admin().connect_to([buyer, seller], env)
    Admin().start_system()
        ```

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
```

To define plans it is also really simple, it only needs the @pl decoration. This decoration must contain the plan change {gain, lose or test}, the data that changed {Belief(s) or Goal(s)} and optionally a context needed to be true to execute the plan {Belief(s) or Goal(s)}.

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')

    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)
    def foo(self,src, *changed_data.values, *context.values):
```

Resuming the the driver example, you can implement a plan the following way:

```py
from maspy import *

class Driver(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("park"))

    # This plan will be executed whenever the agent gains the goal "checkPrice"
    # Every plan needs at least self and src, plus the arguments from the trigger and context
    # for this plan, the context is the belief of a budget with wanted and max prices
    @pl(gain,Goal("checkPrice", Any),Belief("budget",(Any, Any)))
    def check_price(self, src, given_price, budget):
        ...

driver = Driver("Drv")
```

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

Allowing agents to interact with an environment

from maspy import *
import random as rnd
from time import sleep

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

class Manager(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agt_name=None):
        super().__init__(agt_name,read_all_mail=True)
        self.add(Belief("spotPrice",15,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Belief("minPrice",10,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("broadcast_price"))
        self.print("Manager initialized")
        self.end_counter = 0
    
    @pl(gain,Goal("broadcast_price"), Belief("spotPrice",Any))

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

TITLE: Defining Plans

To define plans it is also really simple, it only needs the @pl decoration. This decoration must contain the plan change {gain, lose or test}, the data that changed {Belief(s) or Goal(s)} and optionally a context needed to be true to execute the plan {Belief(s) or Goal(s)}.

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')
    changed_data: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal
    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)
    def foo(self,src, *changed_data.values, *context.values):
```

Resuming the the driver example, you can implement a plan the following way:

```py
from maspy import *

class Driver(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("park"))

    # This plan will be executed whenever the agent gains the goal "checkPrice"
    # Every plan needs at least self and src, plus the arguments from the trigger and context
    # for this plan, the context is the belief of a budget with wanted and max prices
    @pl(gain,Goal("checkPrice", Any),Belief("budget",(Any, Any)))
    def check_price(self, src, given_price, budget):
        ...

driver = Driver("Drv")
```

ID: wiki_09_environment
SOURCE: maspy_wiki
TAGS: environment, perception, event, agent, admin
TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

Allowing agents to interact with an environment

SOURCE: maspy_examples
TITLE: Parking Negotiation Example
CONTENT:
from maspy import *
import random as rnd
from time import sleep

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

class Manager(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agt_name=None):
        super().__init__(agt_name,read_all_mail=True)
        self.add(Belief("spotPrice",15,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Belief("minPrice",10,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("broadcast_price"))
        self.print("Manager initialized")
        self.end_counter = 0
    
    @pl(gain,Goal("broadcast_price"), Belief("spotPrice",Any))

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

Allowing agents to interact with an environment

from maspy import *
import random as rnd
from time import sleep

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

class Manager(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agt_name=None):
        super().__init__(agt_name,read_all_mail=True)
        self.add(Belief("spotPrice",15,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Belief("minPrice",10,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("broadcast_price"))
        self.print("Manager initialized")
        self.end_counter = 0
    
    @pl(gain,Goal("broadcast_price"), Belief("spotPrice",Any))

ID: wiki_05_managing_beliefs_goals
SOURCE: maspy_wiki

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

TITLE: Defining Plans

To define plans it is also really simple, it only needs the @pl decoration. This decoration must contain the plan change {gain, lose or test}, the data that changed {Belief(s) or Goal(s)} and optionally a context needed to be true to execute the plan {Belief(s) or Goal(s)}.

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')
    changed_data: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal
    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)
    def foo(self,src, *changed_data.values, *context.values):
```

Resuming the the driver example, you can implement a plan the following way:

```py
from maspy import *

class Driver(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("park"))

    # This plan will be executed whenever the agent gains the goal "checkPrice"
    # Every plan needs at least self and src, plus the arguments from the trigger and context
    # for this plan, the context is the belief of a budget with wanted and max prices
    @pl(gain,Goal("checkPrice", Any),Belief("budget",(Any, Any)))
    def check_price(self, src, given_price, budget):
        ...

driver = Driver("Drv")
```

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

Allowing agents to interact with an environment

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

To define plans it is also really simple, it only needs the @pl decoration. This decoration must contain the plan change {gain, lose or test}, the data that changed {Belief(s) or Goal(s)} and optionally a context needed to be true to execute the plan {Belief(s) or Goal(s)}.

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')
    changed_data: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal
    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)
    def foo(self,src, *changed_data.values, *context.values):
```

Resuming the the driver example, you can implement a plan the following way:

```py
from maspy import *

class Driver(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("park"))

    # This plan will be executed whenever the agent gains the goal "checkPrice"
    # Every plan needs at least self and src, plus the arguments from the trigger and context
    # for this plan, the context is the belief of a budget with wanted and max prices
    @pl(gain,Goal("checkPrice", Any),Belief("budget",(Any, Any)))
    def check_price(self, src, given_price, budget):
        ...

driver = Driver("Drv")
```

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

Allowing agents to interact with an environment

from maspy import *
import random as rnd
from time import sleep

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

class Manager(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agt_name=None):
        super().__init__(agt_name,read_all_mail=True)
        self.add(Belief("spotPrice",15,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Belief("minPrice",10,adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("broadcast_price"))
        self.print("Manager initialized")
        self.end_counter = 0
    
    @pl(gain,Goal("broadcast_price"), Belief("spotPrice",Any))

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

TITLE: Defining Plans

To define plans it is also really simple, it only needs the @pl decoration. This decoration must contain the plan change {gain, lose or test}, the data that changed {Belief(s) or Goal(s)} and optionally a context needed to be true to execute the plan {Belief(s) or Goal(s)}.

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')
    changed_data: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal
    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)
    def foo(self,src, *changed_data.values, *context.values):
```

Resuming the the driver example, you can implement a plan the following way:

```py
from maspy import *

class Driver(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("park"))

    # This plan will be executed whenever the agent gains the goal "checkPrice"
    # Every plan needs at least self and src, plus the arguments from the trigger and context
    # for this plan, the context is the belief of a budget with wanted and max prices
    @pl(gain,Goal("checkPrice", Any),Belief("budget",(Any, Any)))
    def check_price(self, src, given_price, budget):
        ...

driver = Driver("Drv")
```

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

Allowing agents to interact with an environment

from maspy import *

        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")

        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")

            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

        self.add(Goal("broadcast_price"))
        self.print("Manager initialized")
        self.end_counter = 0
    
    @pl(gain,Goal("broadcast_price"), Belief("spotPrice",Any))

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:

        ```python
        The task is to create an AI agent that can manage a traffic light system. The agent should be able to perceive the state of the traffic light and coordinate the vehicles accordingly.

Here's how you can implement this:


from maspy import *
from random import choice

class TrafficLightAgent(Agent):
    @pl(gain, Belief("traffic_light", Any, "TrafficLightEnv"))
    def traffic_light(self, src, color):
        vehicles = self.list_agents("Vehicle", "Env", "TrafficLightEnv")
        for vehicle in vehicles:
            self.print(f"Detected traffic light: {color} in env {src} - sending signal to {vehicle}")
            self.send(vehicle, achieve, Goal("crossing_over"), "TrafficLightEnv")

class Vehicle(Agent):
    @pl(gain, Goal("crossing_over"))
    def crossing(self, src):
        self.print(f"Confirmation for crossing by {src}")
        self.cross()
        self.print("Crossing completed")
        self.stop_cycle()

class TrafficLightEnv(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.colors = ["red", "yellow", "green"]
        self.create(Percept("traffic_light", choice(self.colors)))

    def cross(self, agt):
        self.print(f"Agent {agt} is now crossing")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    traffic_light_env = TrafficLightEnv("TrafficLightEnv")
    traffic_light_agent = TrafficLightAgent("TrafficLightAgent", beliefs=Belief("traffic_light", Any, "TrafficLightEnv"))
    for _ in range(3):
        Vehicle()
    Admin().report = True


In this code, we first define the TrafficLightAgent class which has a plan that triggers when it perceives a change in the state of the traffic light. This plan sends a signal to all vehicles (identified by the list_agents method) to start crossing.

The Vehicle class has a plan that triggers when it receives a signal to cross. It then crosses and stops its execution.

The TrafficLightEnv class represents the environment where the traffic light is situated. It initializes with a random color for the traffic light. When a vehicle tries to cross, it calls the cross method which prints a message indicating that the vehicle is crossing.

Finally, we create an instance of the TrafficLightEnv and TrafficLightAgent, create three Vehicle instances, and set the report flag in the Admin class to True. This will print out all events that occur during the execution of the system.
        ```

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )

agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

TITLE: Defining Plans

To define plans it is also really simple, it only needs the @pl decoration. This decoration must contain the plan change {gain, lose or test}, the data that changed {Belief(s) or Goal(s)} and optionally a context needed to be true to execute the plan {Belief(s) or Goal(s)}.

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')
    changed_data: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal
    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)
    def foo(self,src, *changed_data.values, *context.values):
```

Resuming the the driver example, you can implement a plan the following way:

```py
from maspy import *

class Driver(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("park"))

    # This plan will be executed whenever the agent gains the goal "checkPrice"
    # Every plan needs at least self and src, plus the arguments from the trigger and context
    # for this plan, the context is the belief of a budget with wanted and max prices
    @pl(gain,Goal("checkPrice", Any),Belief("budget",(Any, Any)))
    def check_price(self, src, given_price, budget):

driver = Driver("Drv")
```

        ```python
        from maspy import *

class CoinWorld(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("coin", (1,), adds_event=False))
        self.create(Percept("coin", (2,), adds_event=False))
        self.create(Percept("coin", (3,), adds_event=False))
        self.coin_count = 0

    def collect_coin(self, agt, coin_id):
        coin = self.get(Percept("coin", (coin_id,), adds_event=False))
        if coin:
            self.rm(Percept("coin", (coin_id,), adds_event=False))
            self.coin_count += 1
            self.add(Belief("has_coin", (coin_id,), adds_event=False))
            return True
        else:
            return None

class Collector(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("coin_count", (0,), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("collect", (3,), adds_event=False))

    @pl(gain, Goal("collect", (3,)), Belief("coin_count", (Any,)))
    def collect_coins(self, src, target, coin_count):
        if coin_count < target:
            coin_id = 1 + coin_count
            self.env.collect_coin(self, coin_id)
        else:
            self.stop_cycle()

if __name__ == "__main__":

    collector = Collector("Collector")
    Admin().connect_to([collector], env)
        ```

TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

from maspy import *
import random as rnd
from time import sleep

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

SOURCE: maspy_wiki

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

CONTENT:
from maspy import *
from random import choice

class Cross_Manager(Agent):
    @pl(gain, Belief("traffic_light", Any, "Crossing"))
    def traffic_light(self, src, color):
        vehicles = self.list_agents("Vehicle","Env","Cross_Junction")
        for vehicle in vehicles:
            self.print(f"Detected traffic light: {color} in env {src} - sending signal to {vehicle}")
            self.send(vehicle, achieve, Goal("crossing_over"), "Crossing")
    
    @pl(gain, Belief("leaving_junction"))
    def end_watch(self,src):
        self.stop_cycle()

class Vehicle(Agent):
    @pl(gain, Goal("crossing_over"))
    def crossing(self,src):
        self.print(f"Confirmation for crossing by {src}")
        self.cross()
        self.print("Crossing Completed")
        self.send(src, tell, Belief("leaving_junction"), "Crossing")
        self.stop_cycle()

class Crossing(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.colors = ["red", "yellow", "green"]
        self.create(Percept("traffic_light", choice(self.colors)))
    
    def cross(self, agt):
        self.print(f"Agent {agt} is now crossing")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    cross_channel = Channel("Crossing")
    cross_env = Crossing("Cross_Junction")
    agent_list: list = [Cross_Manager("CrossManager")]
    for _ in range(3):
        agent_list.append(Vehicle())
    Admin().report = True

- [looping_plan] Plan 'unlock_and_open' updates Belief("door_state") over time while re-adding the same goal, so the decorator must use wildcard matching, for example Belief("door_state", (Any,)).

        ```python
        from maspy import *

class DoorWorld(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("door_state", ("locked",), adds_event=False))

    def unlock_door(self):
        door_state = self.get(Percept("door_state", ("locked",)))
        if door_state:
            self.change(door_state, ("unlocked",))

    def open_door(self):
        door_state = self.get(Percept("door_state", ("unlocked",)))
        if door_state:
            self.change(door_state, ("open",))

class DoorAgent(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None, env=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.env = env
        self.add(Belief("has_key", (True,), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Belief("door_state", ("locked",), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("open_door"))

    @pl(gain, Goal("open_door"), Belief("door_state", ("locked",)))
    def unlock_and_open(self, src, door_state):
        self.env.unlock_door()
        self.rm(Belief("door_state", ("locked",)))
        self.add(Belief("door_state", ("closed",), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("open_door"))

    @pl(gain, Goal("open_door"), Belief("door_state", ("closed",)))
    def open_door(self, src, door_state):
        self.env.open_door()
        self.rm(Belief("door_state", ("closed",)))
        self.rm(Goal("open_door"))
        self.stop_cycle()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    env = DoorWorld("DoorWorld")
    agent = DoorAgent("DoorAgent", env)
    Admin().connect_to([agent], env)
    Admin().start_system()
        ```

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.


TITLE: Environment

MASPY also gives an abstraction to model the environment.

Here's how you create a parking lot for the manager and driver from before:

Creating an environment

```py
from maspy import *

class Park(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        # This creates in the environment a percept for connected agents to perceive.
        # This specific percept does not create a event when percieved by an agent 
        self.create(Percept("spot",(1,"free"),adds_event=False))

    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        # The function get gives you percepts from the environment
        # It has various filters to make this search more precise
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        if spot:
            # This function is used to modify the arguments of an percept.
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,driver))

            # You could also remove the old and create the new
            self.create(Percept("spot",(spot_id,driver)))

    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",("ID",driver)))
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
```

Allowing agents to interact with an environment

from maspy import *
import random as rnd

class Parking(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name,nr_spots=10):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("sold_spots",0,"Spots",False))
        self.print(f"Starting parking with {nr_spots} spots")
        for n in range(1,nr_spots+1):
            self.create(Percept("spot",(n,"free"),"Spots",False))
    
    def park_spot(self, agt, spot_id):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(spot_id,"free")))
        self.print(f"Driver {agt} parking on spot({spot_id})")
        if spot:
            self.change(spot,(spot_id,[agt]))
            return True
        else:
            self.print(f"Requested spot({spot_id}) unavailable")
            return None
    
    def leave_spot(self, agt):
        spot = self.get(Percept("spot",(Any,[agt])))
        if spot:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} leaving spot({spot.values[0]})")
            self.change(spot,(spot.values[0],"free"))
        else:
            self.print(f"Driver {agt} not found in any spot")

        OUTPUT
        - ONLY Python code
        - First line: from maspy import *
        - No comments, prose, markdown, or extra text
        - Include a normal Python entrypoint using if __name__ == "__main__":
        - Only output FAIL if the task is fundamentally impossible or self-contradictory under the MASPY subset below
        - If a previous attempt failed, repair it instead of outputting FAIL

        CORE
        - Use the MASPY subset shown in REFERENCE CONTEXT
        - Do NOT invent APIs, helpers, or shortcuts
        - Follow BDI: goals/beliefs trigger plans
        - Favor the smallest runnable implementation that satisfies the task

        PLANS
        - Only inside Agent classes
        - Only use: @pl(gain, ...)
        - Allowed in @pl: Goal(...), Belief(...) ONLY
        - NEVER use Percept(...) in @pl
        - NO keyword arguments in @pl
        - Plan body may call Environment helper methods through self.env
        - MASPY plan decorator shape is: @pl(change, changed_data, context)
        - After gain, use one trigger term and at most one context argument
        - If more than one context condition is needed, wrap them in a list as the third argument
        - Never write a fourth positional argument in @pl(...); MASPY will ignore it and the plan signature will break
        - If a plan is intended to run repeatedly while values change over time, never hardcode those changing values in Goal(...) or Belief(...) inside the decorator
        - For changing Goal/Belief values, use wildcard matching with Any, for example Goal("collect", (Any,)) and Belief("coin_count", (Any,))
        - Any is allowed in Goal(...) and Belief(...), but never in Percept(...)

        SIGNATURE RULE (CRITICAL)
        - Plan must be: def name(self, src, ...)
        - Include ONE parameter per value contained in changed_data and context Goal/Belief terms
        - Goal values come before Belief context values
        - Goal/Belief terms with no values contribute no parameter
        - If context is a list, count values across every item in the list

        Examples:
        @pl(gain, Goal("turn_on"), Belief("light_state", ("off",)))
        → def turn_light_on(self, src, light_state)

        @pl(gain, Goal("collect", (3,)), Belief("coin", (1,)))
        → def collect_coin(self, src, target, coin_value)

        @pl(gain, Goal("patrol"), [Belief("patrol_point", (Any,)), Belief("alert_state", ("pending",))])
        → def patrol(self, src, patrol_point, alert_state)

        - NEVER mismatch parameters

        PERCEPTS
        - Environment owns percept creation and percept mutation
        - Inside Environment classes, self.create(...), self.add(...), self.rm(...), self.get(...), and self.change(...) must operate on Percept(...), not Belief(...) or Goal(...)
        - Agents should not create or change Percept objects directly
        - NEVER use Any
        - NEVER use values_tuple=
        - Do NOT use True or False inside Percept values; encode state with strings instead

        BELIEFS
        - Use add(...) and rm(...)
        - Remove old belief before adding updated one
        - Use Python variables for counters
        - If a looping goal still has work left, re-add that goal
        - If the current looping goal has finished, stop, terminate, or return without calling self.rm(Goal(...)) on the same trigger goal
        - If the task says to stop or terminate after success, the success branch must explicitly call self.stop_cycle()
        - Do NOT rely on a goal being cleared automatically to stop the program
        - Do NOT use True or False inside Belief or Goal values; encode state with strings like "pending", "sent", "locked", "unlocked", "detected"

        FORBIDDEN
        get_percepts, self.get_percepts, update_belief, get_belief,
        set_belief, delete_belief, has_belief,
        self.world, world., Admin().get_environment(),
        self.update(, self.delete(, delete(,
        wait(, sleep(, log(, self.change(

        RUNTIME
        - Exactly ONE Environment class
        - At least ONE Agent class
        - Instantiate the environment in the entrypoint
        - Instantiate each agent with the environment and store it as self.env
        - Use Admin().connect_to([...agents...], env) before Admin().start_system()

        CONSTRAINTS
        - Do NOT scan percepts unless shown in CONTEXT
        - Do NOT invent environment APIs
        - Use ONLY demonstrated patterns
        - Keep implementation minimal and correct
        - NO extra features, logging, randomness

        IMPLEMENTATION PATTERN
        - Put task state owned by the world into the Environment as plain Python attributes.
        - Expose one small Environment method for each state-changing action the agent must perform.
        - Let plans trigger from Goal(...) and Belief(...) updates, not percept decorators.
        - For counter tasks: read the current belief value, call the environment helper, rm the old belief, add the new belief, then either re-add the goal or stop.
        - Re-add a goal only when more work is still required.
        - When a plan updates a belief that appears in its decorator context, that decorator context should usually use Any rather than a hardcoded literal.
        - When the final success state is reached, call self.stop_cycle() in that success path so the run finishes before timeout.
        - Represent boolean-like state symbolically using strings, not Python booleans inside MASPY terms.

        CRITICAL SIGNATURE LAW
        - MASPY uses @pl(change, changed_data, context).
        - The plan function must be:
        def plan_name(self, src, <changed_data values>, <context values>)
        - If there are multiple context conditions, they must be provided inside a list in the third decorator argument.
        - Every value inside the trigger and context terms contributes one positional argument.
        - If a Goal or Belief has no values, it contributes no extra argument.

        TASK
        - Environment name: DeliveryWorld
- Agent name: Courier
- One Courier agent
- Target: deliver 2 packages
- Initial belief: Belief("delivered_count", (0,))
- Initial belief: Belief("at_location", ("depot",))

- the new delivered_count belief is added with the incremented value
- the agent returns to the depot if more packages remain
- If delivered_count is less than 2, re-add the deliver_packages goal
- Stop only after 2 packages have been delivered

        ```python
        from maspy import *

class DeliveryWorld(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("package", ("depot", "package1"), adds_event=False))
        self.create(Percept("package", ("depot", "package2"), adds_event=False))
        self.create(Percept("location", ("depot",), adds_event=False))
        self.create(Percept("location", ("delivery1",), adds_event=False))
        self.create(Percept("location", ("delivery2",), adds_event=False))

    def pick_up_package(self, agent, package_id):
        package = self.get(Percept("package", (agent.env.name, package_id)))
        if package and package.values[1] == "depot":
            self.change(package, (agent.env.name, "picked_up"))
            return True
        return False

    def deliver_package(self, agent, package_id, location):
        package = self.get(Percept("package", (agent.env.name, package_id)))
        if package and package.values[1] == "picked_up":
            self.change(package, (agent.env.name, "delivered"))
            self.create(Percept("package", (location, package_id), adds_event=False))
            return True
        return False

class Courier(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("delivered_count", (0,), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Belief("at_location", ("depot",), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("deliver_packages", (2,), source=self))

    @pl(gain, Goal("deliver_packages", (Any,)), Belief("delivered_count", (Any,)))
    def deliver_packages(self, src, target, delivered_count):
        if delivered_count < 2:
            package_id = f"package{delivered_count + 1}"
            if self.env.pick_up_package(self, package_id):
                self.rm(Belief("delivered_count", (delivered_count,), adds_event=False))
                self.add(Belief("delivered_count", (delivered_count + 1,), adds_event=False))
                self.add(Goal("deliver_packages", (target,), source=self))
            else:
                self.stop_cycle()
        else:
            self.stop_cycle()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    env = DeliveryWorld()
    courier = Courier("Courier")
    Admin().connect_to([courier], env)
    Admin().start_system()
        ```

        - Do NOT invent APIs, helpers, or shortcuts
        - Follow BDI: goals/beliefs trigger plans
        - Favor the smallest runnable implementation that satisfies the task

        PLANS
        - Only inside Agent classes
        - Only use: @pl(gain, ...)
        - Allowed in @pl: Goal(...), Belief(...) ONLY
        - NEVER use Percept(...) in @pl
        - NO keyword arguments in @pl
        - Plan body may call Environment helper methods through self.env
        - MASPY plan decorator shape is: @pl(change, changed_data, context)
        - After gain, use one trigger term and at most one context argument
        - If more than one context condition is needed, wrap them in a list as the third argument
        - Never write a fourth positional argument in @pl(...); MASPY will ignore it and the plan signature will break
        - If a plan is intended to run repeatedly while values change over time, never hardcode those changing values in Goal(...) or Belief(...) inside the decorator
        - For changing Goal/Belief values, use wildcard matching with Any, for example Goal("collect", (Any,)) and Belief("coin_count", (Any,))
        - Any is allowed in Goal(...) and Belief(...), but never in Percept(...)

        SIGNATURE RULE (CRITICAL)
        - Plan must be: def name(self, src, ...)
        - Include ONE parameter per value contained in changed_data and context Goal/Belief terms
        - Goal values come before Belief context values
        - Goal/Belief terms with no values contribute no parameter
        - If context is a list, count values across every item in the list

        Examples:
        @pl(gain, Goal("turn_on"), Belief("light_state", ("off",)))
        → def turn_light_on(self, src, light_state)

        @pl(gain, Goal("collect", (3,)), Belief("coin", (1,)))
        → def collect_coin(self, src, target, coin_value)

        @pl(gain, Goal("patrol"), [Belief("patrol_point", (Any,)), Belief("alert_state", ("pending",))])
        → def patrol(self, src, patrol_point, alert_state)

        - NEVER mismatch parameters

        PERCEPTS
        - Environment owns percept creation and percept mutation
        - Inside Environment classes, self.create(...), self.add(...), self.rm(...), self.get(...), and self.change(...) must operate on Percept(...), not Belief(...) or Goal(...)
        - Agents should not create or change Percept objects directly
        - NEVER use Any
        - NEVER use values_tuple=
        - Do NOT use True or False inside Percept values; encode state with strings instead

        BELIEFS
        - Use add(...) and rm(...)
        - Remove old belief before adding updated one
        - Use Python variables for counters
        - If a looping goal still has work left, re-add that goal
        - If the current looping goal has finished, stop, terminate, or return without calling self.rm(Goal(...)) on the same trigger goal
        - If the task says to stop or terminate after success, the success branch must explicitly call self.stop_cycle()
        - Do NOT rely on a goal being cleared automatically to stop the program
        - Do NOT use True or False inside Belief or Goal values; encode state with strings like "pending", "sent", "locked", "unlocked", "detected"

        FORBIDDEN
        get_percepts, self.get_percepts, update_belief, get_belief,
        set_belief, delete_belief, has_belief,
        self.world, world., Admin().get_environment(),
        self.update(, self.delete(, delete(,
        wait(, sleep(, log(, self.change(

        RUNTIME
        - Exactly ONE Environment class
        - At least ONE Agent class
        - Instantiate the environment in the entrypoint
        - Instantiate each agent with the environment and store it as self.env
        - Use Admin().connect_to([...agents...], env) before Admin().start_system()

        CONSTRAINTS
        - Do NOT scan percepts unless shown in CONTEXT
        - Do NOT invent environment APIs
        - Use ONLY demonstrated patterns
        - Keep implementation minimal and correct
        - NO extra features, logging, randomness

        IMPLEMENTATION PATTERN
        - Put task state owned by the world into the Environment as plain Python attributes.
        - Expose one small Environment method for each state-changing action the agent must perform.
        - Let plans trigger from Goal(...) and Belief(...) updates, not percept decorators.
        - For counter tasks: read the current belief value, call the environment helper, rm the old belief, add the new belief, then either re-add the goal or stop.
        - Re-add a goal only when more work is still required.
        - When a plan updates a belief that appears in its decorator context, that decorator context should usually use Any rather than a hardcoded literal.
        - When the final success state is reached, call self.stop_cycle() in that success path so the run finishes before timeout.
        - Represent boolean-like state symbolically using strings, not Python booleans inside MASPY terms.

        CRITICAL SIGNATURE LAW
        - MASPY uses @pl(change, changed_data, context).
        - The plan function must be:
        def plan_name(self, src, <changed_data values>, <context values>)
        - If there are multiple context conditions, they must be provided inside a list in the third decorator argument.
        - Every value inside the trigger and context terms contributes one positional argument.
        - If a Goal or Belief has no values, it contributes no extra argument.

        TASK
        - Environment name: MarketEnv
- Agent name: Buyer
- Agent name: Seller
- One Buyer agent
- One Seller agent
- Target: complete a successful purchase through communication
- Buyer initial belief: Belief("budget", (10,))
- Buyer initial goal: Goal("buy_item", ("apple",))
- Seller initial belief: Belief("price", ("apple", 8))
- Seller initial belief: Belief("stock", ("apple", 1))
- The environment manages item availability
- The Buyer must attempt to buy one apple
- The Buyer must communicate with the Seller to request the item
- The Seller must respond with the price
- If the Buyer budget is sufficient:
- the Buyer must send a message to achieve the purchase
- the Seller must reduce stock after the sale
- the Buyer gains Belief("owns", ("apple",))
- the Buyer removes the buy_item goal
- If the item is purchased:
- the Seller updates stock from 1 to 0
- the Buyer retains a belief showing ownership
- Stop only after the Buyer owns the apple and the Seller stock is 0

        - Keep any working structure from the previous attempt.
        - Fix every issue below before changing anything else.
        - Do not output FAIL just because the previous attempt had errors.
        - Output the full corrected file only.

        STATIC FAILURES
        - [plan_signature] Plan 'buy_item' has the wrong signature. Expected def buy_item(self, src, ...) with 2 trigger/context value argument(s), but found 1.
- [looping_plan] Plan 'buy_item' removes Goal("buy_item") even though that goal is the current trigger. For looping goals, re-add it if work remains; otherwise stop or return without self.rm(Goal(...)).

        ```python
        from maspy import *

class MarketEnv(Environment):
    def __init__(self, env_name=None):
        super().__init__(env_name)
        self.create(Percept("item_available", ("apple", 1), adds_event=False))

    def sell_item(self, buyer, item):
        if self.get(Percept("item_available", (item, 1))):
            self.change(self.get(Percept("item_available", (item, 1))), (item, 0))
            self.create(Percept("item_sold", (buyer, item), adds_event=False))
            return True
        return False

class Buyer(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("budget", (rnd.randint(6, 10), rnd.randint(12, 20)), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("buy_item"))

    @pl(gain, Goal("buy_item"), Belief("budget", (Any, Any)))
    def buy_item(self, src, budget):
        if self.env.get(Percept("item_available", ("apple", 1))):
            self.send(src, achieve, Goal("reduce_stock", ("apple",)))
            self.rm(Goal("buy_item"))
            self.stop_cycle()

class Seller(Agent):
    def __init__(self, agent_name=None):
        super().__init__(agent_name)
        self.add(Belief("stock", (1, "free"), adds_event=False))
        self.add(Goal("respond_to_request"))

    @pl(gain, Goal("respond_to_request"), Belief("stock", (Any, Any)))
    def respond_to_request(self, src, buyer):
        if self.env.get(Percept("item_available", ("apple", 1))):
            self.send(src, achieve, Goal("reduce_stock", ("apple",)))
            self.send(buyer, achieve, Goal("receive_item", ("apple",)))
            self.rm(Goal("respond_to_request"))
            self.stop_cycle()

    @pl(gain, Goal("reduce_stock"), Belief("stock", (Any, Any)))
    def reduce_stock(self, src):
        self.env.sell_item(src, "apple")
        self.rm(Goal("reduce_stock"))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    env = MarketEnv()
    buyer = Buyer("Buyer")
    seller = Seller("Seller")
    Admin().connect_to([buyer, seller], env)
    Admin().start_system()
        ```

TITLE: Managing Beliefs and Goals

Here are some info about Beliefs and Goals being created and removed.

- This function adds a goal to the agent;
- The first field represents the goal key and must always be a string;
- The second field represents arguments of the goal and will always be a tuple;
- Each argument can have any structure, with each position of the tuple representing a different one;
- The third field represents the goal source. It is "self" by default, or another agent.
- adding or removing a goal always creates an event for agent, which will try to find a applicable plan.

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
agent.rm( Goal(Name, Values, Source) )
        
agent.add( Goal("check_house", {"Area": [50,100], "Rooms": 5}, ("Seller",27) ) )
agent.add( Goal("SendInfo", ("Information",["List","of","Information",42]) ) )
agent.rm( Goal("walk", source=("trainer",2)) )
```

- This function adds a belief to the agent;
- The first an second field work exaclty the same way as the goal's
- The third field represents the belief source. It is "self" by default, another agent or an environment.
- The fourth field dictates if the adding or removing the belief will generate a new event.
- By default it does, but sometimes one does not want a group of beliefs to be considerend new events

```py
agent = DummyAgent("Ag")
agent.add( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )
agent.rm( Belief(Name, Values, Source, Adds_Event) )

agent.add( Belief("Dirt", (("remaining",3),[(2,2),(3,7),(5,1)])) )
agent.rm( Belief("spot",("A",4,"free"),"Parking",False) )
agent.add( Belief("velocity",57) )
```

```py
    change: TypeVar('gain'|'lose'|'test')

    context: Iterable[Belief | Goal] | Belief | Goal

    @pl(change, changed_data, context)

```

```py
from maspy import *

        self.add(Belief("budget",(rnd.randint(6,10),rnd.randint(12,20)),adds_event=False))
```
